title: Double buffer copy in TX path: lichen_l2_send linearizes, lora_l2_tx copies again
description:
TX path performs two memcpy operations for the same payload:

1. lichen_l2.c:291-298 - net_pkt_read() copies packet into tx_ipv6_buf
2. lora_l2.c:511 - memcpy(tx_buf, data, len) copies frame into internal buffer

For a 200-byte payload, this is 400 bytes of copying. At the current ~1 packet/second rate this is negligible, but the pattern scales poorly if TX rate increases.

The second copy exists because 'Zephyr's lora_send() takes a non-const pointer because some radio drivers may modify the buffer' (lora_l2.c:506-509). This is defensive but may be unnecessary for the specific LoRa driver in use.

What goes wrong: 2x memory bandwidth for TX. On MCUs with slow SRAM, this adds latency and wastes energy.

Potential fix: If the specific LoRa driver (SX126x) is known not to modify the buffer, the copy could be eliminated. Alternatively, lichen_lora_l2_tx() could accept a flag indicating whether the caller's buffer is already sacrificable.
close_reason: Intentional design. Both copies are necessary: (1) lichen_l2_send linearizes fragmented net_pkt into contiguous buffer for SCHC compression - unavoidable since net_pkt uses scatter-gather. (2) lichen_lora_l2_tx copies to internal buffer because Zephyr's lora_send takes non-const pointer - some radio drivers modify the buffer. See lora_l2.c lines 68-70 comment documenting this rationale.
